canonical_summary:
Nuclear compliance in India is governed by a tightly held federal regime built around three institutions: the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB), established 15 November 1983 under Section 27 of the Atomic Energy Act, 1962; the Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), set up by Presidential Order on 3 August 1954 and reporting directly to the Prime Minister; and the Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL), incorporated in September 1987 as the sole licensed operator of grid-connected commercial reactors. Together they regulate, finance and operate the country's 24-reactor fleet across seven sites totalling 8,780 MWe.

The compliance regime rests on the Atomic Energy Act, 1962 (Act 33 of 1962) and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act, 2010 (Act 38 of 2010). Section 14 of the 1962 Act kept reactor build, fuel cycle and operation closed to private parties for more than six decades. Section 17(b) of the 2010 Act introduced a statutory right of recourse against suppliers for defective equipment, an outlier from the IAEA Convention on Supplementary Compensation that stalled the Jaitapur and Kovvada projects for over a decade.

The 2024 to 2026 reform arc has changed both pillars. The SHANTI Act, 2025, assented on 20 December 2025, repeals both statutes, opens licensing to Indian-incorporated private companies and joint ventures, removes Section 17(b), and tiers operator liability between Rs 100 crore and Rs 3,000 crore by reactor capacity.

faqs.question: Who regulates nuclear power plants in India?
faqs.answer: The Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) is the principal regulator. It was constituted on 15 November 1983 by a Presidential notification issued under Section 27 of the Atomic Energy Act, 1962. AERB derives its mandate from Sections 16, 17 and 23 of the 1962 Act and from rules under the Environmental (Protection) Act, 1986. It reports to the Atomic Energy Commission and is headquartered at Niyamak Bhavan, Anushaktinagar, Mumbai. Its remit covers nuclear power plants, research reactors, fuel-cycle facilities, radiation facilities in medicine and industry, transport of radioactive materials, decommissioning and waste management.

faqs.question: What is the legal basis for nuclear compliance in India?
faqs.answer: The framework statute is the Atomic Energy Act, 1962 (Act 33 of 1962), assented on 15 September 1962. Section 3 grants general powers to the Central Government over production, use and disposal of atomic energy. Section 14 is the licensing pillar covering mines, prescribed substances and plant. Section 17 enables radiological safety rules. Section 18 restricts disclosure of nuclear information. Civil liability is governed by the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act, 2010. Both statutes are scheduled to be replaced by the SHANTI Act, 2025, which received presidential assent on 20 December 2025.

faqs.question: What are the stages of the AERB licensing process for a nuclear power plant?
faqs.answer: AERB's consenting process under safety code AERB/NPP/SC/G (Rev. 1) is staged. Each stage requires an independent authorisation before the next can begin. The five stages are siting consent, construction consent, commissioning consent (issued separately for fuel loading, first approach to criticality, low-power physics experiments and power raising), operation licence based on the Final Safety Analysis Report, and decommissioning consent. Operation licences are renewed approximately every five years on the basis of a Periodic Safety Review. Reviews flow through the PDSC, PSRC, ACPSR and finally the Board.

faqs.question: What is Section 17(b) of the CLNDA and why did it stall foreign reactor deals?
faqs.answer: Section 17(b) of the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act, 2010 gave the operator a statutory right of recourse against a supplier where a nuclear incident resulted from supply of equipment or material with patent or latent defects, or from sub-standard services. Unlike the IAEA Convention on Supplementary Compensation, which channels liability exclusively to the operator, Section 17(b) created a non-waivable supplier exposure. Westinghouse, GE-Hitachi and EDF treated this as uninsurable in their home markets and cited it as the principal reason the Jaitapur and Kovvada projects stalled for over a decade.

faqs.question: How many operating nuclear power plants does India have?
faqs.answer: Per NPCIL, India operates 24 reactors across seven sites for a total installed capacity of 8,780 MWe. The sites are Tarapur (Maharashtra), Rawatbhata (Rajasthan), Madras at Kalpakkam (Tamil Nadu), Narora (Uttar Pradesh), Kakrapar (Gujarat), Kaiga (Karnataka) and Kudankulam (Tamil Nadu). The fleet includes pressurised heavy water reactors of 220, 540 and 700 MWe ratings, the original Tarapur boiling water reactors of 160 MWe each, and two Russian-supplied VVER-1000 units at Kudankulam. Rawatbhata Unit 7, a 700 MWe indigenous PHWR, was declared commercial in April 2025.

faqs.question: Can private companies own and operate nuclear power plants in India?
faqs.answer: Under the Atomic Energy Act, 1962, no. Section 14 reserved reactor build, fuel-cycle activity and operation to the Central Government and its wholly owned companies, principally NPCIL and BHAVINI. The SHANTI Act, 2025, which received presidential assent on 20 December 2025, changes this. It permits Indian-incorporated private companies and joint ventures between government entities and private companies to apply for licences. Foreign-incorporated companies remain excluded from ownership, though foreign participation through Indian JVs is permitted. The earlier Bharat Small Reactor model already allowed private capital under NPCIL operatorship without statutory change.

faqs.question: What is the SHANTI Act, 2025?
faqs.answer: The Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India Act, 2025 is the new framework legislation that repeals both the Atomic Energy Act, 1962 and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act, 2010. It was introduced in the Lok Sabha on 15 December 2025, passed by the Lok Sabha on 17 December, passed by the Rajya Sabha by voice vote on 18 December, and received presidential assent on 20 December 2025. It gives AERB statutory standing, opens licensing to private operators and joint ventures, tiers operator liability between Rs 100 and 3,000 crore by reactor capacity, and removes Section 17(b) recourse against suppliers.

faqs.question: What are the AERB site selection criteria for a nuclear power plant?
faqs.answer: Site selection is governed by the Code of Practice on Safety in Nuclear Power Plant Siting (AERB/NPP/SC/S) and its associated guides. The framework requires an Exclusion Zone of 1.5 km radius under operator control, a Sterilised Zone to 5 km with restricted population growth, and an Emergency Planning Zone to 16 km. Population must be evaluated up to 30 km. Geology and seismology assessments cover active faults and the Safe Shutdown Earthquake and Operating Basis Earthquake. Hydrology, meteorology with at least one year of on-site data, external man-induced events, environmental impact and emergency response feasibility complete the criteria.

faqs.question: What are India's principal nuclear safety codes?
faqs.answer: AERB publishes four mandatory codes for nuclear power plants. AERB/NPP/SC/S (Rev. 1) is the Code of Practice on Safety in Nuclear Power Plant Siting. AERB/NPP/SC/D (Rev. 1) is the Code of Practice on Design for Safety in PHWR-based plants. AERB/NPP-PHWR/SC/O (Rev. 1) is the Code of Practice on Safety in Operation. AERB/NPP/SC/QA (Rev. 1) is the Code of Practice on Quality Assurance. These are supplemented by safety guides on seismic input, meteorological dispersion, in-service inspection, fire protection, ageing management and emergency preparedness. The codes draw on IAEA Safety Standards and are reviewed by the ACNS and ACCGAM.

faqs.question: What is the India Nuclear Insurance Pool?
faqs.answer: The India Nuclear Insurance Pool (INIP) was launched on 12 June 2015 by General Insurance Corporation of India and 11 domestic insurers with an initial capacity of Rs 1,500 crore. It was the government's attempt to address foreign vendor concerns over Section 17(b) without amending the statute. INIP issues two policies: an Operator's Policy covering the operator's Section 6(2) liability and a Supplier's Policy, available from 2018, covering liability arising from the operator's right of recourse under Section 17. India also ratified the IAEA Convention on Supplementary Compensation on 4 February 2016.

faqs.question: What is the Nuclear Energy Mission?
faqs.answer: Announced in the Union Budget Speech of 1 February 2025, the Nuclear Energy Mission has a research outlay of Rs 20,000 crore. It commits the Government of India to operationalising at least five indigenously developed Small Modular Reactors by 2033 and a national target of 100 GW of nuclear capacity by 2047, against approximately 8 GW installed in 2024. The Mission was the policy basis for the parliamentary commitment to amend both the Atomic Energy Act and the CLNDA, fulfilled by the SHANTI Act, 2025. PIB releases 2098367 and 2099244 record the framework.

faqs.question: What is the Bharat Small Reactor and how does its compliance pathway work?
faqs.answer: The Bharat Small Reactor (BSR) is a 220 MW Pressurised Heavy Water Reactor design that NPCIL is offering for captive industrial use. NPCIL issued the Request for Proposals on 19 September 2024 under PIB release 2089048. Under the BSR model, the private industrial sponsor provides land, cooling water and capital, while NPCIL retains design, quality assurance, fuel supply and operation and maintenance, and remains the licensed operator. DAE confirmed no statutory amendment was needed for this captive pathway, making BSRs the first commercially deployable channel for private capital into Indian nuclear under the existing Atomic Energy Act.

meta_description: The regulatory architecture, statutes and 2024 to 2026 reforms that govern nuclear compliance in India. AERB, DAE, NPCIL, the Atomic Energy Act and SHANTI.
meta_title: Nuclear compliance in India: AERB, statutes, SHANTI Act

sections.heading: The regulatory architecture: AERB, DAE and NPCIL
paragraphs: Nuclear compliance in India is the responsibility of three federal institutions, each with a distinct constitutional and statutory footing. The Department of Atomic Energy (DAE) was established by Presidential Order on 3 August 1954 and functions under the direct charge of the Prime Minister. The Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) was constituted on 15 November 1983 by a notification of the President of India issued under Section 27 of the Atomic Energy Act, 1962. The Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL) was registered as a public limited company under the Companies Act, 1956 in September 1987 and is the sole licensed operator of the country's grid-connected commercial reactors. NPCIL is the entity that holds AERB authorisations and signs off on regulatory submissions; BHAVINI plays the same role for the Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor at Kalpakkam.

AERB is headquartered at Niyamak Bhavan, Anushaktinagar, Mumbai and reports to the Atomic Energy Commission of India. Its mission is that the use of ionising radiation and nuclear energy in India does not cause undue risk to the health of workers, members of the public, and to the environment. Its authority is drawn from Sections 16, 17 and 23 of the Atomic Energy Act, 1962, and from the Environmental (Protection) Act, 1986. Safety reviews escalate through the Project Design Safety Committee, the Project Safety Review Committee for Operating Plants, the Advisory Committee for Project Safety Review and finally the Board.

subsections.heading: Scope of AERB regulation
bullets: Nuclear power plants (PHWRs, BWRs, fast breeder reactors and light water reactors).
Research reactors and critical facilities operated by DAE.
Fuel-cycle facilities: uranium mining and milling, fuel fabrication, reprocessing and waste management.
Heavy water plants and other DAE industrial plants.
Radiation facilities in medicine, industry, research and agriculture.
Transport of radioactive materials and decommissioning of facilities.
Industrial safety in DAE units under the Factories Act, with AERB as competent authority.

subsections.heading: Where each institution sits
bullets: DAE: policy, R&D and fuel cycle. Apex body under the Prime Minister.
AERB: independent safety regulator. Issues codes, guides and consents.
NPCIL: sole commercial operator of grid-connected nuclear power plants.
BHAVINI: operator of the Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor at Kalpakkam.
BARC: principal R&D centre, designs the indigenous PHWR-700.
NFC, HWB, UCIL: fuel fabrication, heavy water and uranium supply.

sections.heading: The Atomic Energy Act, 1962
paragraphs: The Atomic Energy Act, 1962 (Act 33 of 1962, assented 15 September 1962) is the framework legislation that has governed the sector for more than six decades. Hosted on the India Code repository at indiacode.nic.in and reproduced by AERB, it supplanted the 1948 Act and reflected Homi Bhabha's design of a state-led nuclear estate run by the Department of Atomic Energy under the direct charge of the Prime Minister. Four sections do most of the regulatory work.

subsections.heading: Section 3, general powers of the Central Government
paragraphs: Section 3(a) authorises production, development and use of atomic energy and research into connected matters. Section 3(d) permits the Centre to declare a prohibited area for research, design and production activity. Section 3(e) gives control over radioactive substances and radiation generating plant for prevention of hazards, protection of public safety and safe disposal of radioactive waste.

subsections.heading: Section 14, control over production and use
paragraphs: Section 14(1) is the licensing pillar. It empowers the Centre to prohibit, except under a licence, the working of mines yielding prescribed substances; the acquisition, production, possession, use, disposal, export or import of prescribed substances; and the same activities for plant designed for atomic energy production or for prescribed equipment. Prescribed substances include uranium, plutonium, thorium, beryllium and deuterium. Section 14 is what kept the fuel cycle and reactor operation legally closed to anyone outside the Central Government and its wholly owned companies.

subsections.heading: Section 17, special provisions as to safety
paragraphs: Section 17 empowers the Centre to make rules for premises where radioactive substances are produced, mined, treated, stored or used, or where radiation generating plant or appliances are operated. It covers radiation protection for workers and the public, safe waste disposal, worker qualifications, hours of employment and periodic medical examination. On contravention, the Centre may seal premises and seize radioactive substances. Section 17 is the statutory hook used by AERB for worker protection.

subsections.heading: Section 18, restriction on disclosure
paragraphs: Section 18(1) permits the Central Government to restrict disclosure of information in any form relating to existing or proposed atomic energy plant, its purpose or method of operation, or any process operated in it. Section 18(2) bars persons from disclosing or attempting to obtain such information. Section 18(3) carves out narrow exceptions for non-atomic uses. Prosecutions under Section 18 require the consent of the Attorney General.

sections.heading: The AERB licensing process
paragraphs: The consenting process for a nuclear power plant is staged and progressive. Each stage requires an independent authorisation from AERB before the next can begin. The framework is laid out in the safety code AERB/NPP/SC/G (Rev. 1), Regulation of Nuclear and Radiation Facilities. Safety codes are mandatory; safety guides are recommended methods; safety manuals and standards supplement both. The codes draw on IAEA Safety Standards and are reviewed by the Advisory Committee on Nuclear Safety (ACNS) and the Advisory Committee for Codes, Guides and Associated Manuals (ACCGAM).

subsections.heading: The five consenting stages
bullets: Siting consent: review of the site evaluation report, environmental impact assessment and demonstration that the site meets AERB/NPP/SC/S criteria.
Construction consent: review of the Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR), design documents and quality assurance programmes.
Commissioning consent: issued in sub-stages for fuel loading, first approach to criticality, low power physics experiments and power raising.
Operation licence: granted after review of the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and technical specifications. Renewed approximately every five years on the basis of a Periodic Safety Review.
Decommissioning consent: required at end of plant life.

subsections.heading: Principal codes and site criteria
bullets: AERB/NPP/SC/S (Rev. 1): Code of Practice on Safety in Nuclear Power Plant Siting.
AERB/NPP/SC/D (Rev. 1): Code of Practice on Design for Safety in PHWR-based plants.
AERB/NPP-PHWR/SC/O (Rev. 1): Code of Practice on Safety in Nuclear Power Plant Operation.
AERB/NPP/SC/QA (Rev. 1): Code of Practice on Quality Assurance for Safety in Nuclear Power Plants.
Exclusion Zone: 1.5 km radius under operator control. Sterilised Zone: 5 km. Emergency Planning Zone: 16 km. Population evaluation to 30 km.
Site guides cover atmospheric dispersion (SG/S-1), extreme meteorological values (SG/S-3), design basis flood (SG/S-6A), seismic design basis (SG/S-11) and human-induced events (SG/S-7).

sections.heading: Operating plants today
paragraphs: Per NPCIL, the operating fleet comprises 24 reactors across seven sites totalling 8,780 MWe of installed capacity. Pressurised Heavy Water Reactors dominate the fleet because India was cut off from enriched uranium supplies after the 1974 nuclear test, leaving natural uranium fuelled, heavy water moderated and cooled reactors as the indigenous workhorse. The PHWR-700 is the current standard for fleet-mode build, with passive decay heat removal, a steel-lined containment and a partial boiling regime that lifted output from 540 to 700 MWe.

subsections.heading: Sites and units
bullets: Tarapur (TAPS), Maharashtra: Units 1 and 2, 160 MWe BWRs (1969, GE-supplied under US cooperation); Units 3 and 4, 540 MWe indigenous PHWRs (2005, 2006).
Rawatbhata (RAPS), Rajasthan: Unit 2 (200 MWe PHWR, 1981); Units 3 to 6 (220 MWe PHWRs, 2000 to 2010); Unit 7 (700 MWe PHWR, declared commercial April 2025).
Madras (MAPS), Kalpakkam, Tamil Nadu: Units 1 and 2, 220 MWe PHWRs (1984, 1986). First fully Indian-designed PHWRs.
Narora (NAPS), Uttar Pradesh: Units 1 and 2, 220 MWe PHWRs (1991, 1992).
Kakrapar (KAPS), Gujarat: Units 1 and 2, 220 MWe PHWRs (1993, 1995); Units 3 and 4, 700 MWe indigenous PHWRs (2023, 2024).
Kaiga (KGS), Karnataka: Units 1 to 4, 220 MWe PHWRs (2000 to 2011).
Kudankulam (KKNPS), Tamil Nadu: Units 1 and 2, Russian VVER-1000 PWRs at 1,000 MWe each (2014, 2017). Units 3, 4, 5 and 6 under construction.

subsections.heading: Construction and fleet-mode expansion
bullets: Rawatbhata 8, Kaiga 5 and 6 and the Gorakhpur (GHAVP) units in Haryana under fleet-mode construction.
Kudankulam 3 to 6 will make the site India's largest nuclear station once complete.
BHAVINI's Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor at Kalpakkam is the demonstration platform for the second stage of the Indian three-stage programme.

sections.heading: Civil nuclear liability: CLNDA 2010 and Section 17(b)
paragraphs: The Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act, 2010 (Act 38 of 2010) was notified by the Ministry of Law and Justice on 21 September 2010 and gave effect to India's accession to the IAEA Convention on Supplementary Compensation for Nuclear Damage. Section 4 establishes strict no-fault liability of the operator. Section 6(1) caps total per-incident liability at the rupee equivalent of 300 million Special Drawing Rights, approximately Rs 3,167 crore at recent SDR rates. Section 6(2) caps the operator's own liability at Rs 1,500 crore, with Section 7(1)(a) requiring the Centre to bridge any gap up to the SDR ceiling. The Nuclear Damage Claims Commission, established under Sections 19 to 38, is the adjudicating forum.

subsections.heading: Section 17(b): the contested clause
paragraphs: Section 17 gives the operator a right of recourse where the right is expressly provided in a written contract (clause a), where the incident resulted from supply of equipment or material with patent or latent defects or sub-standard services (clause b), or where it resulted from an act done with intent to cause nuclear damage (clause c). Clause (b) is the outlier. Unlike the IAEA Convention and the Vienna and Paris Conventions, which limit supplier exposure to wilful misconduct or contractually agreed terms, Section 17(b) creates a statutory, non-waivable hook. Westinghouse, GE-Hitachi and EDF / Framatome cited it as the principal reason Jaitapur (Maharashtra) and Kovvada (Andhra Pradesh) stalled for more than a decade after the 2008 India to US civil nuclear agreement.

subsections.heading: The 2015 to 2018 insurance-pool workaround
paragraphs: On 12 June 2015 General Insurance Corporation of India (GIC Re) and 11 domestic insurers launched the India Nuclear Insurance Pool (INIP) with a capacity of Rs 1,500 crore. INIP issues an Operator's Policy covering Section 6(2) liability and a Supplier's Policy, available from 2018, covering liability arising only from the operator's right of recourse under Section 17. India deposited its instrument of ratification of the CSC with the IAEA on 4 February 2016. The DAE position was that INIP cover, a contractual-conditionality reading of Section 17 and CSC ratification together resolved foreign vendor concerns. In practice, vendors continued to demand a statutory fix.

sections.heading: The 2024 to 2026 private sector reforms
paragraphs: The reform arc has four dated milestones. On 23 July 2024 Union Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman, in the full-year Budget Speech for FY 2024-25, announced for the first time that the Government would partner with the private sector on Bharat Small Reactors, on R&D for the Bharat Small Modular Reactor, and on newer technologies. On 19 September 2024 NPCIL issued a Request for Proposals for 220 MW Bharat Small Reactors for captive industrial use under PIB release 2089048. On 1 February 2025 the Budget Speech for FY 2025-26 launched the Nuclear Energy Mission with a Rs 20,000 crore outlay, a target of at least five indigenously developed SMRs by 2033 and 100 GW of nuclear capacity by 2047, and committed to amend both the Atomic Energy Act and the CLNDA.

subsections.heading: The SHANTI Act, 2025
paragraphs: The Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India Bill, tracked by PRS Legislative Research, was introduced in the Lok Sabha on 15 December 2025, passed by the Lok Sabha on 17 December, passed by the Rajya Sabha by voice vote on 18 December (with an opposition walkout demanding Standing or Joint Committee referral), and received presidential assent on 20 December 2025. The Act repeals both the Atomic Energy Act, 1962 and the CLNDA, 2010 and consolidates them into the Nuclear Energy Act.
bullets: Indian-incorporated private companies and joint ventures may apply for licences to build, own and operate nuclear facilities. Foreign-incorporated companies are excluded from direct ownership; foreign participation flows through Indian JVs.
AERB is given statutory standing for the first time.
An Atomic Energy Redressal Advisory Council is created.
Operator liability is tiered between Rs 100 crore and Rs 3,000 crore by reactor capacity.

Section 17(b) recourse against suppliers for defective equipment is removed. Supplier exposure is limited to express contractual warranties and wilful misconduct or gross negligence, aligning with CSC Annex Article 10.
The Patents Act, 1970 is amended to permit patents for peaceful nuclear inventions.

subsections.heading: Two JV architectures now visible
bullets: BSR captive model under the existing framework: private industrial off-taker funds the plant; NPCIL is the licensed operator. Targeted at decarbonising steel, aluminium and refining loads. No statutory amendment required.

Greenfield IPP model under the new Nuclear Energy Act: consortia (reported interest from NTPC, ONGC, Reliance, Tata, Adani and Vedanta) may take licences directly, with NPCIL as a possible minority technical partner. This is the channel for the BSMR (300 MW class) and for conventional large PHWR and LWR builds aimed at 100 GW by 2047.

sections.heading: What foreign vendors need to know
paragraphs: For vendors who paused at the door of the Indian market after 2010, the compliance picture in 2026 is materially different. Section 17(b) of the CLNDA has been removed by the SHANTI Act. Supplier liability is now bounded by express contractual warranties and by wilful misconduct or gross negligence, which is the position under the IAEA Convention on Supplementary Compensation. India has been a CSC contracting party since 4 February 2016 and the India Nuclear Insurance Pool remains available to backstop residual exposure. Operator liability is tiered between Rs 100 crore and Rs 3,000 crore by reactor capacity, with state-backed top-up under the CSC structure.

subsections.heading: Routes to participate
bullets: BSR captive route: contract with NPCIL as licensed operator under the existing Atomic Energy Act framework, available since the 19 September 2024 RFP.
Greenfield IPP route: contract or take equity in an Indian-incorporated licensee or JV under the SHANTI Act, with foreign participation through Indian JVs.
Bharat Small Modular Reactor R&D: participate in the Nuclear Energy Mission Rs 20,000 crore research programme aimed at five SMRs by 2033.
Conventional large reactor builds: pursue licensing for PHWR and LWR projects targeting 100 GW by 2047, with NPCIL as a possible minority technical partner.

subsections.heading: Compliance touchpoints unchanged
bullets: AERB consenting remains staged: siting, construction, commissioning (in sub-stages), operation and decommissioning. The Board itself issues the consent.
Mandatory codes AERB/NPP/SC/S, /SC/D, /SC/O and /SC/QA continue to govern siting, design, operation and quality assurance.
Section 14 licensing under the 1962 Act remains the operative framework until the Nuclear Energy Act commencement notification, after which licensing migrates to the SHANTI regime.
DAE retains policy ownership; NPCIL remains the largest operator and the natural JV partner for technology transfer.
Section 18-type disclosure restrictions and prohibited-area designations survive in updated form, so technical information flows still require Central Government authority.
